questions.questionText:
1. We use the put method in the HTTP request message for doing partial updates.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: The PUT method is designed to completely replace a target resource. For partial updates, the PATCH method is the correct standard.

questions.questionText:
2. To determine the type of an object, we can use the "typeof" function in JavaScript.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: While technically `typeof` is an operator rather than a function (often written without parentheses like `typeof x`), it is widely accepted in basic web technology contexts as the primary way to determine a variable's type. Note: for specific object types, `instanceof` is more accurate, but `typeof` returns 'object'.

questions.questionText:
3. CSS can be defined externally in a separate file by adding the <style> element inside the <head>.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: External CSS is linked using the `<link rel="stylesheet" href="...">` element. The `<style>` element is used exclusively for internal (embedded) CSS.

questions.questionText:
4. var colors = (1:"red", 2:"green", 3:"blue") is one of the ways of defining an array in JavaScript.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: This syntax is entirely invalid in JavaScript. Arrays are defined using square brackets `[]` (e.g., `["red", "green", "blue"]`), and objects are defined using curly braces `{}`.

questions.questionText:
5. Django views represents the presentation layer of Django.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: In Django's MVT (Model-View-Template) architecture, the View represents the business logic/controller layer. The Template represents the presentation layer.

questions.questionText:
6. In Python, format function is used to format the datatypes of the output.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: The `format()` string method is used to control the string presentation and insert variables into string placeholders, not to change or format underlying data types themselves.

questions.questionText:
7. In Python, A set is a collection which is both unordered and indexed.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: A set in Python is unordered and unindexed. You cannot access set items by referring to an index or a key.

questions.questionText:
8. We can register models in the admin.py file to view it in the administration site of Django.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: Using `admin.site.register(ModelName)` inside `admin.py` is the standard way to make models visible and editable in the Django admin interface.

questions.questionText:
9. "Post.Objects.all()" will retrieve all objects in the Post table.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: Python is case-sensitive. The correct syntax uses a lowercase 'o': `Post.objects.all()`.

questions.questionText:
10. The super() function in python enables a class to have unlimited number of attributes.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: The `super()` function is used to return a proxy object that delegates method calls to a parent or sibling class, allowing inheritance. It has nothing to do with enabling an unlimited number of attributes.

questions.questionText:
11. "migrate" command in Django is used to show the changes happened in the database.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: The `migrate` command applies the migrations to the database. To show changes or view migrations, commands like `showmigrations` or checking the output of `makemigrations` are used.

questions.questionText:
12. In Python, to append elements from another list to the current list, use the update() method.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: Lists use the `extend()` method to append elements from an iterable. The `update()` method is used for sets and dictionaries.

questions.questionText:
13. In Python, to add items from another set into the current set, use the extend() method.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: Sets use the `update()` method to add items from another set (or any iterable). `extend()` is used for lists.

questions.questionText:
14. Tags and texts that are not directly displayed on the HTML page are written in title section.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: These tags are written in the `<head>` section, not the title section. The `<title>` is merely one element inside the head.

questions.questionText:
15. If an application is running on Web Server Gateway Interface (WSGI), then it will handle client requests in a sequential manner.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: WSGI is generally synchronous and handles requests sequentially per worker. For asynchronous, non-blocking handling, ASGI is used.

questions.questionText:
16. For a model to be visible in the admin interface it has to be registered in the settings.py file.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: Models must be registered in the `admin.py` file, not `settings.py`.

questions.questionText:
17. In DOM, ChildNodes property returns elements and ignores text and comments.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: `childNodes` returns all node types, including text nodes (like whitespace) and comment nodes. The `children` property is what returns only element nodes.

questions.questionText:
18. In python, a set is a collection which is unordered and unindexed.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: This is the precise definition of a standard Python set.

questions.questionText:
19. In python, to create a class that inherits from another class we use inherits keyword.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: Python does not have an `inherits` keyword. Inheritance is achieved by passing the parent class as a parameter in parentheses: `class ChildClass(ParentClass):`.

questions.questionText:
20. In Python, when using the super () function, the child class will not inherit the methods and properties from the parent class.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: This statement is the exact opposite of reality. `super()` is used precisely to access and inherit methods and properties from the parent class.

questions.questionText:
21. In the following statement:
myNumbers = [1,2,3]
x = myNumbers.pop()
The removed item will be 1.
questions.explanation: By default, the `pop()` method in Python removes and returns the *last* item in the list, which would be 3, not 1.

questions.questionText:
22. In the following statement: myNumbers = [1,2,3]
x = myNumbers.pop()
The removed item will be 3.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: `pop()` removes the last element of the list, which is 3.

questions.questionText:
23. x = ("apple", "banana", "cherry") is a definition of a list.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: This defines a tuple, as it uses parentheses `()`. A list uses square brackets `[]`.

questions.questionText:
24. my-var is an incorrect variable name in Python.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: Hyphens are not allowed in Python variable names because they are interpreted as the minus operator. Variable names can only contain letters, numbers, and underscores.

questions.questionText:
25. The CSS property background-attachment is used to specify whether the image is attached to the document or not.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: Yes, `background-attachment` sets whether a background image scrolls with the rest of the page, or if it is fixed (attached).

questions.questionText:
26. The output of the following statements is Hello, My
b = "Hello, My Friend"
print(b[:8])
questions.explanation: The slice `b[:8]` extracts characters from index 0 to 7. Counting from 0: 'H'(0), 'e'(1), 'l'(2), 'l'(3), 'o'(4), ','(5), ' '(6), 'M'(7). The output is 'Hello, M', which is 8 characters long. 'Hello, My' would require `[:9]`.

questions.questionText:
27. Strip() function is used to remove whitespaces from the beginning and end of a string in Python.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: `strip()` safely removes leading and trailing characters (whitespace by default).

questions.questionText:
28. The output of the following Python statements is ['a', 'b', 'C']
X = ['C','b','a']
X.sort()
print(X)
questions.explanation: In Python, default sorting uses ASCII values. Uppercase letters have lower ASCII values than lowercase ones. Therefore, 'C' comes before 'a' and 'b'. The output will be `['C', 'a', 'b']`.

questions.questionText:
29. When you create a Django project on your computer, you will get a folder for your project with a specific content including templates file.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: By default, the `django-admin startproject` command does not generate a `templates` folder or file. The developer must create it manually.

questions.questionText:
30. Django comes with a built-in user interface that allows you to administrate your data. To access this interface you have to create a user name and password. To create this such user, you write python mange.py createuser.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: The correct command to create an administrative user is `python manage.py createsuperuser`, not `createuser` (and manage.py is misspelled in the prompt).

questions.questionText:
31. AJAX is used for refreshing the page content by sending a request to the server and updating the whole HTML page from the response.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: The primary purpose of AJAX is asynchronous updating, allowing *parts* of a web page to be updated without needing to reload the *whole* HTML page.

questions.questionText:
32. The output of the following Python statements is ['a', 'b', 'c']
X = ['C','b','A']
X.sort(key = str.lower)
print(X)
questions.explanation: The `key=str.lower` argument tells the sort function to ignore case during comparisons, but it preserves the original elements. The output will be `['A', 'b', 'C']`.

questions.questionText:
33.
tag is used to force the browser to display the next text or element on a new line. a. True b. False
questions.explanation:
The `
` tag inserts a single line break.

questions.questionText:
34. y= ("apple",) is a tuple.
a. True
b. False
questions.explanation: In Python, a single-element tuple requires a trailing comma. `("apple",)` correctly creates a tuple.

topic: Web Technology Final Exam - Multiple Choice (35-52)

questions.questionText: 35. An HTTP response content type denotes

options.content: The media type of the content
The image size of the content
The text size of the content

questions.explanation: The `Content-Type` entity header is used to indicate the media type (MIME type) of the resource.

questions.questionText: 36. Using authorization, having a response of status 200 means that

options.content: User is not authorized to see the response
User is authorized to see the response
Content cannot be displayed after authorization

questions.explanation: HTTP 200 OK implies the request was successful. In the context of authorization, it implies the server accepted the credentials and fulfilled the request.

questions.questionText: 37. Using authorization, having a response of status 403 means that

options.content: Server is reachable but the user is not authorized to see the response
Server is not reachable, and the user is not authorized to see the response
Server is reachable and no content available to show the user

questions.explanation: HTTP 403 is the 'Forbidden' status code. The client must authenticate itself to get the requested response, or lacks permissions even with authentication.

options.content: allow the function to be waiting for more arguments to be sent.
allow the function to change the arguments to an array.
allow the function to distribute the arguments to separate numbers to be multiplied.

questions.explanation: The rest parameter syntax `...` allows a function to accept an indefinite number of arguments as an array.

questions.questionText: 39. Which of the following is true about JavaScript syntax?

options.content: Semicolons are mandatory at the end of every statement.

questions.explanation: JavaScript is case-sensitive (e.g., `myVar` is different from `myvar`). Semicolons are technically optional due to automatic semicolon insertion.

questions.questionText: 40. What does 'use strict' do in JavaScript?

options.content: Enforces stricter parsing and error-handling rules
Allows use of old JavaScript syntax

questions.explanation: `'use strict';` switches the JavaScript engine to strict mode, throwing errors for bad practices (like using undeclared variables).
In strict mode, assigning a value to an undeclared variable throws a ReferenceError.

questions.questionText: 42. What is the difference between var, let, and const?

options.content: var is block-scoped, let and const are function-scoped.
var is function-scoped, let and const are block-scoped.
var and let are block-scoped, const is function-scoped.

questions.explanation: `var` binds to the nearest enclosing function wrapper, whereas `let` and `const` bind to the nearest enclosing block (`{ ... }`).

questions.questionText: 43. What is the purpose of the prototype property in JavaScript objects?

options.content: To create new properties in an object
To define methods and properties for inheritance
To store the type of the object

questions.explanation: Prototypes are the mechanism by which JavaScript objects inherit features from one another.

questions.questionText: 44. In JavaScript, which of the following is a valid getter in an object?
questions.explanation: The `get` syntax binds an object property to a function that will be called when that property is looked up.
`delete obj.a` removes property `a` from the object. `console.log(obj)` then shows only `{ b: 2 }`. `delete` does not set the value to `null` or `undefined` — the key is gone.

questions.questionText: 46. In JavaScript, What is the purpose of the constructor method in a class?

options.content: To define static properties of a class
To inherit methods from another class

questions.explanation: The `constructor` method is a special method of a class for creating and initializing an object instance of that class.

questions.questionText: 47. In JavaScript, What does the super keyword do?

options.content: Creates a new object in the class

questions.explanation: The `super` keyword is used to access properties on an object literal or class's [[Prototype]], or invoke the superclass's constructor.

questions.questionText: 48. How many ways are there with which we can declare a variable in javascript?
questions.explanation: There are primarily three variable declaration keywords in JavaScript: `var`, `let`, and `const`.

questions.questionText: 49. What does document.querySelectorAll() return?
questions.explanation: `document.querySelectorAll()` returns a static (not live) NodeList representing a list of the document's elements that match the specified group of selectors.

questions.questionText: 50. What does window.location.href return?

options.content: The URL of the current page

questions.explanation: The `href` property of the `Location` interface returns a string containing the entire URL of the current document.

questions.questionText: 51. What is the purpose of window.history.back()?

options.content: To navigate to the previous page in history

questions.explanation: This method loads the previous URL in the history list, functionally identical to the user clicking their browser's "Back" button.

questions.questionText: 52. How do you remove an element from the DOM?
questions.explanation: The `Element.remove()` method removes the element from the DOM tree it belongs to.

context.text: Based on the following HTML page, answer questions from (53-58)
context.code:
Welcome to my page

Welcome

Paragraph 1

Paragraph 2

Paragraph 3

Paragraph 4

Paragraph 5

Hello Again

Paragraph 6.

questions.questionText: 53. What will be the output of `document.getElementById("id1").firstChild.nodeName;`?
questions.explanation: The `firstChild` of `<h2 id="id1">` is the text node containing "Welcome to my page". For a text node, `nodeName` returns `#text`.

questions.questionText: 54. By using this CSS selector p.center {color: blue;}, the number of elements that will be styled is
questions.explanation:
The selector targets `

` tags with the class `center`. Looking at the HTML, only `

Paragraph 5

` matches. The `

` also has class center, but is not a `

` tag. Thus, 1 element.

questions.questionText: 55. By using this CSS selector p:first-child { color: blue;}, how many elements will be styled?
questions.explanation:
This selector targets `

` tags that are the very first child of their parent. The matches are: `

Paragraph 1

` (first child of `
`) and `

Paragraph 3

` (first child of `
`). That is 2 elements. 2 is not an option in the list (a=5, b=6, c=1), so 'None of the previous'.

questions.questionText: 56. By using this CSS selector div + p { color: red;}, how many p elements will be styled?
questions.explanation:
The `+` is the adjacent sibling combinator. It selects the `

` immediately following the `

`. The only one is `

Paragraph 5

`. Since the answer is 1, and the options are 3, 2, 4, it is None of the previous.

questions.questionText: 57. By using this CSS selector p:empty {color:red;}, how many elements will be styled?
questions.explanation:
There are no empty `

` tags in the given HTML. All of them contain text. So 0 elements are styled. None of the options matches.

questions.questionText: 58. If we wrote "var x=document.getElementsByTagName('p')" inside a script tag, then the length of x will be:
questions.explanation:
Counting the `

` elements in the HTML page: Welcome, Paragraph 1, Paragraph 2, Paragraph 3, Paragraph 4, Paragraph 5, and Paragraph 6 gives 7 tags. Since 7 is not listed in options a, b, or c, the answer is None of the previous.

context.text: Based on the following HTML page, answer questions from (59-61)

questions.questionText: 59. The value of x in this code, "x=document.body.children;" is:
questions.explanation: The `.children` property always returns an `HTMLCollection` (a collection of elements), even if it only contains one element (in this case, the `<ol>`).

questions.questionText: 60. If we wrote this code, "y=x[0].children" then y will contain:
questions.explanation: The `.children` property is strictly for element nodes. (Conversely, `.childNodes` would return element nodes AND text nodes like whitespace).

questions.questionText: 61. If we wrote this code, "z=y[0].children; M=z[0].firstChild.nodeValue;" Then the value of M will be:
questions.explanation:
`y[0]` is `
  • `. Its `.children` (`z`) contains the `

    ` tag. `z[0]` is `

    List 1

    `. The `.firstChild` of that `

    ` tag is a text node with the content. Its `.nodeValue` accesses the string content, which is "List 1".

topic: Web Technology Final Exam - Multiple Choice (62-80)

questions.questionText: 62. What is a correct syntax for creating a Django project?
questions.explanation: `django-admin startproject project_name` is the standard terminal command to bootstrap a new Django project.

questions.questionText: 63. If you have a Django application named members, and a model named Member, what is a correct syntax to import the model?
questions.explanation: Django stores app models inside `models.py`. Standard Python import syntax extracts a specific class from that module via `from app_name.models import ClassName`.

questions.questionText: 64. When you have done changes in a model in Django, which command has to be executed in order to make the changes take effect in the database?
questions.explanation: The `makemigrations` command (option c) creates the Python instructions, but to *make the changes take effect* in the database, the `python manage.py migrate` command must be run. Since it is missing, D is the answer.

questions.questionText:
65. In the following Python Statements:
Y={1,1,2,3,3,5}
print(Y)
The output will be:
questions.explanation: `Y` is a set, so duplicate values are removed. `print(Y)` outputs the unique elements `{1, 2, 3, 5}`, which matches option c. Spacing after commas in the printed representation is not required to match exactly.

questions.questionText: 66. What does {{ name }} this mean in Django Templates?

options.content: It will be displayed as name in HTML.
The name will be replaced with values of Python variable.

questions.explanation: In Django's template language, double curly braces evaluate the variable inside and output its value into the template.

questions.questionText:
67. What will be the output of the following Python code?
car= {
"brand": "Ford",
"model": "Mustang",
"year": 1964
}
y= car.get("color", "red")
print(len(y))
questions.explanation: `car.get("color", "red")` returns the default string `"red"` because `color` is not in the dictionary. `len("red")` is 3.
The rest parameter `...args` gathers arguments into a JavaScript Array. In JavaScript, arrays are technically objects, so `typeof(args)` evaluates to 'object'.

questions.questionText: 69. Which method can be used to combine two arrays in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: The `concat()` method is used to merge two or more arrays without altering the existing ones.

questions.questionText:
70. What will be the output of the following python code?
newtuple = (1,2,3,4)
(*k,) = newtuple
print(k)
questions.explanation: The unpacking assignment `(*k,) = newtuple` collects all items of the tuple into a list `k`. So `k` evaluates to `[1, 2, 3, 4]`. Since this isn't listed (b is `[2,3,4]`), the answer is None of the previous.

questions.questionText:
71. What will be the output of the following Python code?
newtuple=(1,2,3)
(x, *k, y, z, m) = newtuple
print(k)
questions.explanation: The unpacking requires at least 4 items (for `x, y, z, m`). The tuple only has 3 items. This results in a `ValueError: not enough values to unpack`.

questions.questionText:
72. Identify the error in this CSS code:
.class { color: blue; font-weight; bold; }
questions.explanation: The property-value pair uses a semicolon instead of a colon. It should be `font-weight: bold;`.

questions.questionText: 73. Why might the CSS rule .container p {color: green;} not apply to a paragraph inside a div with class "container"?

options.content: The div does not have the "container" class
The paragraph is not a direct child
There is a more specific rule overriding this
The CSS file is not linked properly

questions.explanation: CSS resolution depends heavily on specificity. If another rule with a higher specificity (e.g., `#id p` or an inline style) targets the same element, it will override `.container p`.

questions.questionText: 74. If an element has margin: 10px; and padding: 20px;, what is the space between the element and its neighboring element?
questions.explanation: Margin controls the space *outside* an element (between it and its neighbors). Padding controls the space *inside* (between the border and the content). Therefore, 10px.

questions.questionText:
75. In the following Python statements:
Y = set(("a","b","c"))
print(Y)
The output will be:
questions.explanation: In Python 3, printing a set of strings outputs curly braces like `{'a', 'b', 'c'}` (order may vary). None of the options match this exact syntax.

questions.questionText:
76. In the following Python Statements:
Y={1,1,2,3,3,5}
print(Y)
The output will be:
questions.explanation: Duplicates are removed, so the values are `{1, 2, 3, 5}`, but Python 3 prints sets with curly braces (e.g. `{1, 2, 3, 5}`), not `set([1,2,3,5])`. None of the listed formats match exactly.

questions.questionText:
77. The following python code:
x={
"name": "Ahmed",
"id": "1",
"mobile": 123456789,
"mobile": 987654321
}
print(x)
The above print will return:

options.content: Error! As you cannot have duplicates in a dictionary

questions.explanation: Dictionaries in Python cannot have duplicate keys. If you assign to the same key twice during initialization, the last assignment overwrites the previous one.

questions.questionText:
78. What is the output of the following JavaScript snippet?
var writeMe="console.log('document.writeln(x);')"
var x=13
eval(writeMe);

options.content: Writes 13 to the body of the document
Writes "document.writeln(x);" to the body of the document

questions.explanation: `eval()` executes the string as JavaScript. The string is a `console.log()` command printing a literal string. The value of `x` is never actually evaluated inside that inner string.
`.flat(2)` yields `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]`, and `console.log` prints array brackets and spaces. Option c omits brackets, so it does not match the actual console output.

`f()` starts the async function, then `console.log('first!')` runs immediately. After about one second the awaited promise resolves and `done!` is logged. Because both strings appear, the answer is "Something else."
